h1Title: Achromatopsia: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Achromatopsia is a rare, inherited retinal condition in which all three types of cone photoreceptors fail to function, leaving vision mediated entirely by rod photoreceptors. It affects approximately 1 in 30,000 people worldwide and produces a characteristic tetrad: complete inability to perceive color, severely reduced visual acuity (typically 20/200), pendular nystagmus, and intense photophobia in daylight conditions. The condition is present from birth and remains stable throughout life — it does not progress to blindness. Six causative genes have been identified, with CNGA3 and CNGB3 mutations accounting for roughly 75% of all cases. No approved treatment exists, but multiple gene therapy trials are currently in advanced clinical stages, making achromatopsia one of the most actively pursued targets in ocular gene therapy.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 1 in 30,000 people globally (approximately 250,000 worldwide); higher prevalence of 1 in 1,500-10,000 among Pingelapese Islanders of the Caroline Islands due to founder effect
keyStats.demographics: Equal sex distribution; autosomal recessive inheritance; consanguinity increases risk; all racial groups affected equally
keyStats.avgAge: Congenital: present from birth; typically diagnosed at 6-18 months when parents notice nystagmus and light sensitivity; nystagmus may reduce slightly in severity by age 5-7
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 250,000-300,000 people worldwide; CNGA3 mutations predominant in European populations; CNGB3 mutations predominant in Atlantic island founder populations

definition: Achromatopsia (ICD-10: H53.51) is a hereditary cone photoreceptor dysfunction syndrome characterised by the complete absence of functional cone-mediated vision. The fovea — the central retinal region densely packed with red-, green-, and blue-sensitive cone cells — relies entirely on cyclic nucleotide-gated (CNG) channels for phototransduction. Loss-of-function mutations in the genes encoding the alpha or beta subunits of these channels (CNGA3, CNGB3) or other cone phototransduction cascade components (GNAT2, PDE6C, PDE6H, ATF6) abolish cone signalling, leaving only rod photoreceptors functional. Rod vision provides only monochromatic (shades of grey) perception, is most effective at low light levels, and provides lower acuity than cone vision — hence the typical visual acuity of 20/200 to 20/400. Rod function is entirely normal and preserved throughout life, distinguishing achromatopsia from rod-cone dystrophies like retinitis pigmentosa. The condition is also called rod monochromacy to reflect this exclusive reliance on rods. Achromatopsia differs from the far more common partial colour vision deficiencies (red-green colour blindness, affecting 8% of males) which involve dysfunction in one of three cone types, not total cone failure. The stable, non-progressive nature of achromatopsia and the accessibility of cone photoreceptors to subretinal gene delivery have made it a focus of ophthalmological gene therapy research since 2015.

typesClassification.description: Mutations in CNGA3 (encoding the alpha subunit of the cone CNG channel) account for approximately 25-30% of cases in European populations. Phenotype is typical complete achromatopsia. Multiple recurrent mutations identified; c.1148delC and p.R563C are common European alleles.

Mutations in CNGB3 (beta subunit of cone CNG channel) account for approximately 40-50% of cases worldwide; the highest frequencies are in founder populations of the Pacific islands. The founder variant p.T383fsX is responsible for the Pingelap achromatopsia cluster.
Mutations in GNAT2 encoding the alpha subunit of cone transducin, accounting for approximately 2% of cases. Milder phenotype is reported in some series; partial cone function occasionally preserved.

Rare mutations in the cone photoreceptor phosphodiesterase 6 subunits; together account for approximately 5% of molecularly confirmed cases. PDE6C mutations may occasionally present with progressive cone dystrophy features.

Mutations in ATF6, encoding a transcription factor involved in endoplasmic reticulum stress and cone photoreceptor development, cause a clinically distinct form with foveal hypoplasia and variable photoreceptor morphology on OCT. Recognised as a cause of achromatopsia in 2015.

primarySymptoms: Complete inability to distinguish colors — all wavelengths of visible light appear as shades of grey; patients cannot pass the Ishihara pseudoisochromatic colour plate test on any plate.
Severely reduced visual acuity, typically between 20/200 and 20/400 (legal blindness threshold in the US is 20/200), caused by the absence of cone-mediated high-resolution central vision.
Pendular nystagmus — an involuntary, rhythmic, oscillating eye movement present from early infancy that may be horizontal, vertical, or circular; often most pronounced in bright light.

Intense photophobia in bright lighting conditions: daytime glare is severely disabling; patients actively seek shade, squint persistently, and prefer dim light where rod photoreceptors are more efficient.

Day blindness (hemeralopia): paradoxically better visual function in dim light or at dusk, when rod photoreceptors are operating at their optimum sensitivity and the bright-light-induced cone response is absent.
Foveal hypoplasia detectable on optical coherence tomography (OCT): incomplete foveal pit development because cone-photoreceptor-driven centripetal migration during fetal development is disrupted.
Eccentric fixation preference: patients may use peripheral retinal regions (off-foveal fixation) to achieve better visual resolution in some lighting conditions.
Reduced contrast sensitivity and impaired fine detail resolution under daylight conditions due to the absence of high-spatial-frequency cone input.
Normal scotopic (dark-adapted) electroretinogram: rod responses are fully intact, distinguishing achromatopsia from rod-cone dystrophies where rod function is eventually lost.
earlyWarningSigns: Nystagmus noticed in an infant aged 2-4 months that is horizontal-pendular and associated with squinting in daylight — the classic infant presentation preceding formal diagnosis.
A young child who consistently prefers dim rooms, pulls down blinds, and squints severely outdoors while having apparently better vision at dusk or night time.
Failure to identify colors on preschool color-sorting tasks in a child with nystagmus and photophobia, prompting ophthalmic referral.
Family history of consanguinity combined with nystagmus in a newborn should prompt early electroretinography (ERG) to characterise photoreceptor function before age 1.
emergencySigns: Acute change in the pattern of nystagmus or sudden new photophobia beyond the child's established baseline — may suggest a superimposed condition (cataract, glaucoma, uveitis) requiring urgent review.
Severely reduced visual acuity below the child's established baseline — indicates possible progression to a related rod-cone dystrophy or development of an additional ocular condition.

Any parent or patient reporting sudden 'worsening' of colour perception in an established achromatopsia patient — may suggest incomplete diagnosis of progressive cone dystrophy rather than true achromatopsia, requiring re-evaluation.

causes.description: Loss-of-function mutations in CNGB3 encoding the beta subunit of the cone-specific cyclic nucleotide-gated channel abolish the channel's ability to open in response to cGMP produced during phototransduction. Without functional channels, cone photoreceptors cannot generate electrical signals in response to light. The frameshift mutation p.T383fsX accounts for the vast majority of cases in Pingelap founder populations and is enriched in Atlantic island populations through genetic drift.

Loss-of-function mutations in CNGA3 encoding the alpha subunit of the same cone CNG channel cause the same complete loss of cone photoresponse. CNGA3 mutations occur at a higher frequency in European and some Middle Eastern populations. Over 60 pathogenic CNGA3 variants have been reported; the molecular defect is autosomal recessive in all cases.

Mutations in GNAT2 (cone transducin alpha subunit), PDE6C (cone phosphodiesterase alpha prime subunit), and PDE6H (cone phosphodiesterase gamma prime subunit) all disrupt the cone phototransduction cascade at steps downstream of the photopigment but upstream of the CNG channel. All are inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern and produce phenotypically similar complete achromatopsia.

causes.cause: ATF6 mutations (a distinct endoplasmic reticulum mechanism)
causes.description: Mutations in the transcription factor ATF6, which regulates unfolded protein response and endoplasmic reticulum stress in photoreceptors, cause cone photoreceptor aplasia or severe hypoplasia through impaired cone development rather than channel dysfunction. Foveal hypoplasia on OCT is often more pronounced in ATF6-related cases.

Achromatopsia is autosomal recessive, meaning two copies of a pathogenic variant are required. Consanguineous unions between relatives sharing the same recessive allele substantially increase the probability of homozygous offspring. Geographical founder effects (Pingelap Island, some isolated alpine communities in Germany) have raised local prevalence by orders of magnitude above the general population rate.

riskFactors.description: Two carrier parents each carrying one pathogenic allele have a 25% chance of an affected child per pregnancy. Carrier frequency for CNGB3 and CNGA3 combined is approximately 1 in 100 in the general population, consistent with a disease prevalence of roughly 1 in 30,000.

Children of first-cousin unions are 6-7 times more likely to be homozygous for rare recessive alleles. Consanguinity is a major contributing factor in achromatopsia prevalence in Middle Eastern and South Asian populations.

Pingelapese Islanders of the Caroline Islands in Micronesia carry a CNGB3 founder variant at a carrier frequency of approximately 30%; the disease prevalence in this population approaches 1 in 12, compared with 1 in 30,000 globally.
CNGA3 mutations are enriched in European and some Middle Eastern populations through population-specific allele frequencies. Screening within confirmed-carrier European families is warranted.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of nystagmus and photophobia in infancy
riskFactors.description: A sibling or parent with congenital nystagmus and photophobia in a family known to carry an achromatopsia-causing variant warrants early genetic testing and ERG evaluation in subsequent offspring.

affectedDemographics: Affects approximately 1 in 30,000 people of all racial backgrounds worldwide; no sex predisposition (autosomal recessive inheritance).

Pingelapese Islanders of the Caroline Islands in Micronesia have the highest known prevalence, approximately 1 in 12, due to a severe bottleneck event after a typhoon in the 18th century that reduced the population to fewer than 20 survivors, one of whom was a CNGB3 carrier.
European populations have a higher relative frequency of CNGA3 mutations; Middle Eastern and South Asian populations see higher absolute rates from consanguineous marriages.
Most diagnoses are made in the first 1-2 years of life based on nystagmus and photophobia; the average diagnostic delay in Western countries is 1.5-2 years from symptom onset.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis is made through a combination of clinical examination, electrophysiology, retinal imaging, and genetic testing. The characteristic clinical presentation — congenital nystagmus, profound photophobia, severely reduced visual acuity, and complete absence of colour discrimination — is highly suggestive. The diagnostic gold standard is the full-field electroretinogram (ERG): achromatopsia produces absent or severely reduced photopic (cone) responses under light-adapted conditions, with intact scotopic (rod) responses under dark-adapted conditions, distinguishing it clearly from rod-cone dystrophies that impair both systems. Colour vision testing confirms complete achromacy — patients fail all Ishihara plates except plates 1 and 2 (which can be read by rods alone), and the anomaloscope shows an extremely widened colour-match range inconsistent with partial colour deficiency. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) reveals foveal hypoplasia — a shallow or absent foveal pit — and, in severe cases, cone photoreceptor depletion in the fovea. Adaptive optics imaging can demonstrate the absence of cone mosaic structure at the foveal centre in research settings. Genetic testing of the CNGA3, CNGB3, GNAT2, PDE6C, PDE6H, and ATF6 genes achieves molecular confirmation in approximately 75-80% of clinically diagnosed cases; panels are available from accredited clinical genetics laboratories. Molecular diagnosis is increasingly important for gene therapy trial eligibility and genetic counselling. The differential includes blue cone monochromacy (X-linked, only short-wavelength cones present, different ERG), cone-rod dystrophy (progressive, normal infant development then decline), and Leber congenital amaurosis (rod involvement from birth, nystagmus, and no scotopic response).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard physiological test; confirms absent cone responses (photopic ERG flat) with intact rod responses (scotopic ERG normal), the defining pattern of complete achromatopsia
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Electrodes are placed on the corneal surface (contact lens electrode) or near the eye after pupil dilation. The patient views flashing lights at different intensities and light adaptations. Performed under sedation in young children. Results available same day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates complete colour discrimination failure across all wavelengths; anomaloscope confirms achromacy distinguished from partial colour vision deficiency
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Ishihara plates are colour dot images viewed from 75 cm; the patient identifies numbers or patterns. The anomaloscope presents a side-by-side colour match task. Takes 10-15 minutes. No drops required.

diagnosticTests.test: Optical coherence tomography (OCT) of the fovea
diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates foveal hypoplasia (shallow or absent foveal pit), cone photoreceptor depletion in the foveal centre, and outer segment structure; evaluates cone layer thickness for gene therapy eligibility
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A camera scans the retina from a fixed distance; patient stares at a fixation light. No contact required; takes 5-10 minutes. Cross-sectional retinal images show layer-by-layer detail of the photoreceptor structure.

diagnosticTests.test: Genetic testing panel (CNGA3, CNGB3, GNAT2, PDE6C, PDE6H, ATF6)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies causative mutation, confirms diagnosis, guides genetic counselling, and establishes eligibility for gene therapy clinical trials
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw or saliva sample. DNA is extracted and sequenced against known pathogenic variants. Results in 4-8 weeks from a clinical genetics laboratory. Biallelic pathogenic variants confirm diagnosis in approximately 75-80% of clinically diagnosed cases.

diagnosticTests.test: Best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) with low-vision assessment
diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents functional vision level and guides provision of low-vision aids; typically 20/200-20/400 in achromatopsia
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard chart-based acuity testing with trial frames under standardised illumination; low-vision assessment adds contrast sensitivity testing. Takes 20-30 minutes including refraction.

diagnosticTests.test: Pupillary light reflex assessment and photophobia quantification
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms normal pupillary constriction mediated by intrinsically photosensitive retinal ganglion cells (ipRGCs), which are intact in achromatopsia, and quantifies photophobia severity using standardised light-exposure protocols
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard neurological examination finding; the ophthalmologist shines a light and observes the pupil. Photophobia quantification uses calibrated light sources and patient-reported discomfort scores at different illumination levels.

treatmentOverview: No approved pharmacological or gene therapy treatment for achromatopsia is available as of 2026, but this is the most rapidly changing area in the field. Symptom management focuses on reducing photophobia, maximising functional vision, and providing low-vision rehabilitation. Red-tinted or dark grey tinted spectacle lenses (photochromic or fixed tint) attenuate broad-spectrum short-wavelength light that is particularly bothersome to rod-mediated vision; red filter contact lenses or 'X-chrom' lenses are used by some patients to reduce photophobia further, though they do not restore colour perception. Dark, wraparound sunglasses in outdoor settings dramatically improve daily comfort. Magnification — hand-held or spectacle-mounted — compensates for reduced visual acuity. Low-vision aids including large-print reading materials, screen magnification software, and text-to-speech technology extend functional independence. Electronic glasses incorporating real-time image processing to enhance contrast and reduce glare are an emerging assistive technology. The gene therapy landscape is the most dynamic aspect of achromatopsia management. Phase I/II trials using recombinant AAV2 or AAV8 vectors carrying corrective CNGA3 or CNGB3 cDNA delivered by subretinal injection have demonstrated safety and some evidence of efficacy in adult patients in trials conducted by multiple groups in Germany, the UK, and the US (ABCA4 Foundation and Beacon Therapeutics). Cone photoreceptor survival — dependent on structural integrity of cone outer segments on OCT — is the main determinant of gene therapy eligibility; younger patients and those with preserved cone photoreceptor layers have the best predicted response. Nystagmus does not fully resolve but may reduce in amplitude following successful cone function restoration in animal models.

medicalTreatments.name: Red or dark grey tinted spectacle lenses (FL-41, red filter)
medicalTreatments.description: Spectacle or contact lenses with red, amber, or dark tinting selectively filter short-wavelength and broad-spectrum visible light that overstimulates rod photoreceptors. FL-41 (rose-tinted) and deep red filters reduce photophobia substantially; wraparound frames minimise peripheral glare exposure in outdoor settings.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Subjective photophobia scores improved by 40-60% in achromatopsia patient surveys; no randomised trial data, but widely adopted as standard supportive care

medicalTreatments.name: Low-vision rehabilitation and magnification aids
medicalTreatments.description: Hand-held, spectacle-mounted, or electronic magnifiers; large-print and high-contrast materials; screen reader software; text-to-speech tools; and orientation-and-mobility training for functional independence. Bioptic telescopes can improve distance acuity for some patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Structured low-vision rehabilitation programs improve functional independence scores in visually impaired populations; benefit is individualised based on degree of vision loss and patient goals

medicalTreatments.name: Subretinal AAV gene therapy (clinical trial stage — CNGA3 and CNGB3)
medicalTreatments.description: Recombinant AAV vectors carrying functional CNGA3 or CNGB3 cDNA are injected beneath the retina near the fovea in a day-case procedure under general or local anaesthesia. The virus transduces surviving cone photoreceptors, restoring CNG channel expression and phototransduction capacity. Phase I/II trials have demonstrated safety; efficacy data in 2024-2025 show partial improvement in cone ERG amplitude and photophobia in some participants.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Phase I/II trial data (ACHM-001 CNGB3, MeiraGTx CNGA3) show acceptable safety profiles; a subset of participants demonstrate improved cone ERG signals and reduced photophobia; full clinical efficacy data from Phase III trials are awaited

surgicalOptions.name: Subretinal gene therapy vector injection (investigational)
surgicalOptions.description: A vitrectomy is performed under sedation or general anaesthesia; a fine cannula delivers the AAV gene therapy vector beneath the foveal photoreceptors via a small retinal bleb. The procedure is performed in one eye per treatment session. Considered surgical in nature due to the intraocular injection approach.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Procedure technical success rates above 95% in reported trials; partial retinal detachment from the injection bleb is expected and typically resolves without complication; no serious sight-threatening adverse events in published Phase I/II reports through 2025

alternativeTreatments.name: Coloured filter contact lenses (X-Chrom lens)
alternativeTreatments.description: A red-tinted contact lens worn over one eye allows differential wavelength detection between eyes, which some patients interpret as a form of colour differentiation through binocular rivalry. No evidence that true colour perception is restored, but photophobia reduction is reported.

alternativeTreatments.name: Augmented reality glasses for real-time colour information
alternativeTreatments.description: Prototype wearable devices process visual scenes and overlay colour labels or convert hue information into sound (chromesthesia). Early feasibility studies show some improvement in colour identification tasks; not yet in commercial production for achromatopsia specifically.

whySeeSpecialist: Paediatric ophthalmologists or adult retinal specialists with expertise in inherited retinal diseases should lead diagnosis and management. Genetic counselling is essential once a molecular diagnosis is made for accurate carrier testing and family planning. Access to ERG and retinal imaging determines which centres can confirm the diagnosis. Gene therapy trial eligibility requires assessment at participating trial centres.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an ophthalmologist specialising in inherited retinal diseases with access to full-field ERG, spectral-domain OCT, and molecular genetics. In the UK, referral to a recognised centre for rare retinal disorders is standard. Patients interested in gene therapy trials should seek referral to a university hospital currently running achromatopsia trials (Germany: Tübingen; UK: Oxford; US: multiple sites). Low-vision rehabilitation input from a qualified low-vision practitioner is equally important.
hospitalCriteria: Inherited retinal disease clinic with full-field ERG capability
Spectral-domain OCT for foveal morphology and cone layer assessment
Clinical genetics service for molecular diagnosis and genetic counselling
Access to national rare disease gene therapy trial programmes
keyFacilities: Specialist inherited retinal disease centres (e.g., Moorfields Eye Hospital, Jules Stein Eye Institute)
Clinical genetics laboratories for CNGA3/CNGB3/GNAT2 gene panel testing
National rare disease centres with gene therapy trial access
preventionStrategies: Carrier couples with confirmed biallelic pathogenic variants in CNGA3 or CNGB3 can access preimplantation genetic testing with IVF to select unaffected embryos.
Prenatal diagnosis by amniocentesis or chorionic villus sampling is available when both parents are confirmed carriers.
Genetic counselling for all first-degree relatives of affected individuals; sibling carrier testing enables informed family planning decisions.
Community genetic screening programmes in high-prevalence founder populations (e.g., Pingelap) can identify carrier couples before pregnancy.
lifestyleModifications: Wear high-quality red-filter wraparound sunglasses outdoors at all times; dark-tinted photochromic lenses adapt to varying light conditions during the school or work day.
Request low-lighting accommodations in workplaces and schools; fluorescent lighting is particularly difficult and should be replaced with warmer, dimmer alternatives where possible.
Use screen magnification software and high-contrast display settings (white text on black background) consistently on all digital devices.
Plan outdoor activities for dawn, dusk, or overcast conditions when rod photoreceptors are working at optimal sensitivity.
Connect with international achromatopsia patient organisations for peer support, trial updates, and adaptive technology recommendations.
Register with a national inherited retinal disease registry to be notified of gene therapy trial openings.

recommended: Foods rich in vitamin A (beta-carotene): orange and yellow vegetables, leafy greens — supports rod photoreceptor rhodopsin synthesis and general retinal health
Omega-3 fatty acids (DHA from oily fish or algae supplements) — essential component of photoreceptor outer segment membranes
Lutein and zeaxanthin (kale, spinach, egg yolk) — macular pigments that filter high-energy short-wavelength light and may protect residual photoreceptor structures
Antioxidant vitamins C and E to protect remaining photoreceptor cells from oxidative stress
avoid: Tobacco smoking — associated with accelerated photoreceptor degeneration in inherited retinal diseases
Excessive alcohol — reduces retinal blood flow and may impair photoreceptor metabolic support
High-dose antioxidant supplementation beyond recommended daily allowances without medical advice in patients concurrently enrolled in gene therapy trials

exerciseGuidelines: No specific restrictions on exercise in achromatopsia. Outdoor sports in bright sunlight require tinted wraparound eyewear. Depth perception may be slightly reduced because of impaired high-acuity central vision; high-speed ball sports and driving a motor vehicle are impaired by the level of visual acuity (20/200 or worse). Swimming, indoor sports, and physical fitness activities are fully accessible.
dailyManagement: Apply FL-41 or deep red-filter spectacles immediately on rising before exposure to indoor lighting or natural light.
Set all digital screens to high-contrast mode, reduced brightness, and enlarged text baseline; phone accessibility settings include large-text mode and colour-inversion options.
Use a brimmed hat outdoors to reduce overhead glare reaching under wraparound spectacle frames.
Schedule high-visual-demand tasks (reading, screens, fine work) for indoor, dim-light conditions where rod function is maximised.
Carry a pocket magnifier for reading labels, restaurant menus, and signs when magnification aids are not otherwise available.
prognosis: Achromatopsia is a stable, non-progressive condition — cone photoreceptors do not function from birth but do not continue to degenerate as in cone-rod dystrophy. Rod vision remains fully intact throughout life, ensuring that night vision and peripheral vision are normal and that total blindness does not occur. Visual acuity measured under standardised photopic conditions remains stable at 20/200 to 20/400 across the lifespan in large natural history cohorts. Photophobia may lessen marginally with adaptive sun-avoidance behaviours and improved tinted lens technology. Quality of life is significantly impacted by educational, occupational, and social limitations from reduced acuity and photophobia, but with appropriate low-vision support and vocational adaptation most individuals with achromatopsia lead productive independent lives. Gene therapy trials now progressing to Phase II/III carry real potential for partial or significant cone function restoration in the coming years, particularly for younger patients with preserved cone photoreceptor structure on OCT.
recoveryTimeline: Achromatopsia is congenital and stable; there is no recovery from the underlying cone dysfunction with current treatments. Nystagmus may diminish slightly in amplitude between ages 5-10 years. Low-vision rehabilitation outcomes improve over 6-12 months of structured training. Gene therapy outcomes, if proven, are expected to stabilise or improve cone ERG responses over 6-24 months post-injection based on preclinical and early clinical data.
complications: Legal blindness from reduced visual acuity — approximately 20/200 in most patients, placing them at or near the legal blindness threshold in most jurisdictions and limiting driving, many occupations, and independent navigation.

Severe photophobia limiting outdoor activities, causing secondary social isolation, school attendance difficulties, and avoidance of natural environments; inadequately managed photophobia is the primary determinant of quality-of-life impairment.
Nystagmus-related reading difficulty and image instability — head-nodding strategies (null point adoption) and near magnification aids partially compensate.

Psychosocial impact including anxiety and depression related to visual disability, particularly during adolescence when peer participation in sports, driving, and social activities is reduced; rates of anxiety and depression are elevated in achromatopsia populations.

Misdiagnosis and diagnostic delay — average 1.5-2 years from first presentation to molecular confirmation, during which families may receive inadequate or incorrect information and miss access to low-vision services.

supportResources.name: Achromatopsia Network
supportResources.description: US and international patient community providing peer support, gene therapy trial updates, and practical resources for individuals with achromatopsia.

supportResources.name: Foundation Fighting Blindness
supportResources.description: US non-profit funding achromatopsia gene therapy research and providing disease information and clinical trial matching.

supportResources.name: Moorfields Eye Hospital Inherited Retinal Disease Service
supportResources.description: UK NHS specialist service for diagnosis, ERG assessment, genetic testing, low-vision rehabilitation, and gene therapy trial access.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acquired colour vision deficiency
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acquired colour vision deficiency develops later in life from optic nerve or retinal disease (glaucoma, AMD, toxic exposure) and affects some colour discrimination rather than all. Achromatopsia is congenital with total absence of colour perception from birth, associated with nystagmus and photophobia from infancy. ERG shows intact cone responses in acquired deficiency but absent cone responses in achromatopsia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Night blindness (nyctalopia)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Night blindness reflects impaired rod photoreceptor function in dim light — the opposite deficit to achromatopsia, where rod function is preserved and it is cone function that is absent. Patients with achromatopsia have paradoxically better vision in dim light. Night blindness is often caused by vitamin A deficiency or retinitis pigmentosa, neither of which underlies achromatopsia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Congenital nystagmus (idiopathic)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Idiopathic infantile nystagmus presents similarly in infancy but without significant photophobia, colour vision loss, or reduced visual acuity below 20/80. ERG is normal in idiopathic infantile nystagmus. Achromatopsia nystagmus is accompanied by the full tetrad of photophobia, total colour blindness, and acuity below 20/200, with a flat photopic ERG.

relatedConditions.name: Acquired colour vision deficiency
relatedConditions.relevance: The acquired form of colour vision loss caused by retinal or optic nerve disease; key differential from congenital achromatopsia.

relatedConditions.name: Night blindness
relatedConditions.relevance: Rod photoreceptor dysfunction condition contrasting with achromatopsia; understanding the distinction between rod and cone disorders is central to differential diagnosis.

relatedConditions.name: Age-related macular degeneration
relatedConditions.relevance: Both affect foveal cone photoreceptors and impair central acuity; AMD is the leading acquired cause of central vision loss and shares the foveal anatomy targeted by gene therapy.

relatedConditions.name: Diabetic retinopathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Progressive retinal disease that causes acquired colour vision deficiency and central vision loss in advanced stages; shares OCT and ERG monitoring modalities with achromatopsia.

faqs.question: What is achromatopsia?
faqs.answer: Achromatopsia is a rare inherited condition in which all three types of cone photoreceptors in the retina fail to function. It causes complete inability to perceive colour, severely reduced visual acuity (typically 20/200), intense photophobia in bright light, and pendular nystagmus. It affects approximately 1 in 30,000 people and is present from birth.

faqs.question: Is achromatopsia the same as colour blindness?
faqs.answer: No. Common red-green colour blindness affects 8% of men and involves reduced discrimination of two colour channels. Achromatopsia is a total absence of all colour perception, affecting only 1 in 30,000 people. It also causes severely reduced visual acuity, photophobia, and nystagmus — symptoms absent in ordinary colour blindness.

faqs.question: Can people with achromatopsia see at night?
faqs.answer: Yes. People with achromatopsia rely entirely on rod photoreceptors, which function best in dim light. They typically see better at dusk and in dim environments than in bright daylight — the opposite pattern of most visual impairments. This day blindness in bright light and preserved dim-light vision is called hemeralopia.

faqs.question: What causes achromatopsia?
faqs.answer: Achromatopsia is caused by autosomal recessive mutations in genes encoding cone photoreceptor proteins: CNGB3 (40-50% of cases), CNGA3 (25-30%), GNAT2, PDE6C, PDE6H, and ATF6. Mutations abolish the cone phototransduction cascade. Both parents must carry a copy of the mutated gene for a child to be affected.

faqs.question: Does achromatopsia get worse over time?
faqs.answer: No. Achromatopsia is stable throughout life. Cone photoreceptors do not function from birth but do not progressively degenerate as in cone-rod dystrophy. Rod vision remains intact permanently. Visual acuity and photophobia stay at the same level throughout adulthood, and total blindness does not occur.

faqs.question: Is there a treatment for achromatopsia?
faqs.answer: No approved treatment restores cone function as of 2026. Symptom management uses red-filter spectacles for photophobia and magnification aids for visual acuity. Gene therapy clinical trials using AAV vectors carrying CNGA3 or CNGB3 are in Phase I/II stages and have shown safety and early signs of efficacy; Phase III trials are underway.

faqs.question: How is achromatopsia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis uses full-field electroretinography (flat photopic/cone ERG with intact scotopic/rod ERG), colour vision testing (failure of all Ishihara plates), OCT showing foveal hypoplasia, and genetic panel testing. Molecular confirmation via CNGB3, CNGA3, GNAT2, PDE6C, PDE6H, or ATF6 sequencing is achieved in approximately 75-80% of clinically diagnosed cases.

faqs.question: What is the visual acuity of someone with achromatopsia?
faqs.answer: Typical best-corrected visual acuity in achromatopsia is 20/200 to 20/400 in bright light conditions. This places most affected individuals at or near the legal blindness threshold (20/200 in most jurisdictions). Vision is better in dim light, where rod acuity may reach 20/100-20/150 in optimal conditions.

faqs.question: Can a child with achromatopsia go to a mainstream school?
faqs.answer: Yes, with appropriate support. Most children with achromatopsia benefit from low-vision aids, large-print materials, high-contrast displays, reduced classroom lighting, and permission to wear tinted lenses indoors. Early involvement of a visual impairment specialist teacher ensures appropriate educational accommodations. Many children with achromatopsia attend mainstream school successfully.

faqs.question: What is the difference between achromatopsia and colour blindness?
faqs.answer: Achromatopsia is a total loss of all colour vision from complete cone photoreceptor failure, combined with photophobia, nystagmus, and reduced acuity to 20/200. Red-green colour blindness is a partial colour discrimination deficit affecting 8% of men with normal acuity and no photophobia. Achromatopsia is far rarer and far more disabling.

faqs.question: What glasses help with achromatopsia?
faqs.answer: Red or rose-tinted spectacles (FL-41 filter) or dark grey wraparound lenses reduce the photophobia and glare that impair function in bright light. Some patients use a deep red filter contact lens in one eye. Tinted lenses do not restore colour perception but significantly reduce light sensitivity and improve outdoor comfort and functional vision.

faqs.question: Is achromatopsia hereditary?
faqs.answer: Yes. Achromatopsia is autosomal recessive — a child must inherit one pathogenic gene variant from each parent to be affected. Parents who are both carriers of the same gene variant have a 25% chance of an affected child per pregnancy. Siblings of affected individuals have a 25% chance of being affected and a 50% chance of being unaffected carriers.

faqs.question: What is the achromatopsia gene therapy trial?
faqs.answer: Multiple Phase I/II trials are testing subretinal injection of AAV vectors carrying corrective CNGA3 or CNGB3 gene copies. The vector transduces surviving cone photoreceptors to restore CNG channel expression and cone phototransduction. Published results through 2025 confirm safety and partial cone ERG restoration in some participants; Phase III trials are enrolling.

faqs.question: Do people with achromatopsia dream in colour?
faqs.answer: Most reports from people with congenital achromatopsia indicate they do not experience colour in dreams, as the visual cortex processes only rod-based greyscale input throughout development. Some individuals report vague colour-like sensations in dreams but cannot identify specific hues. Acquired achromatopsia from brain injury may produce different dream phenomenology.

faqs.question: Can achromatopsia be detected before birth?
faqs.answer: Yes, if both parents are confirmed carriers of causative variants. Prenatal diagnosis is available through amniocentesis or chorionic villus sampling. Preimplantation genetic testing with IVF allows selection of unaffected embryos before implantation. Genetic counselling before pregnancy is recommended for confirmed carrier couples.

faqs.question: Is nystagmus in achromatopsia treatable?
faqs.answer: Nystagmus in achromatopsia cannot currently be eliminated. It tends to diminish slightly in amplitude between ages 5-10 years. Some patients adopt a null-point head position to reduce oscillation. If gene therapy successfully restores cone function, animal models predict partial nystagmus reduction; this has not yet been robustly demonstrated in human trials.

symptomKeywords: sees in black and white only
better vision at night than day
metaDescription: Achromatopsia: rare inherited cone disorder causing total colour blindness, photophobia, and reduced visual acuity. Symptoms, genetics, gene therapy reviewed.
